does: This paper addresses the problem of applying RLHF when human preferences are heterogeneous rather than uniform, proposing two complementary frameworks. The personalization-based approach learns multiple reward models through representation learning and clustering to capture preference diversity while managing the bias-variance tradeoff from using less data per model. The aggregation-based approach maintains a single reward model while combining heterogeneous feedback through principled preference aggregation. The work provides sample complexity guarantees for both personalization methods and demonstrates how to handle strategic behavior in feedback provision, extending beyond standard RLHF assumptions of homogeneous preferences.
connects: This work builds on the established success of RLHF for language model alignment, which has primarily assumed a single unified reward model reflecting consensus human values. It connects to research on preference learning and human-in-the-loop machine learning that must handle value disagreement. The paper extends classical multi-armed bandit and inverse reinforcement learning frameworks to the heterogeneous preference setting, drawing on clustering and representation learning techniques from unsupervised learning. It also relates to literature on robust aggregation of diverse opinions and strategic reporting, addressing concerns about preference manipulation that arise when collecting feedback from multiple human annotators with conflicting objectives.
enables: This framework enables more realistic deployment of RLHF systems that acknowledge and accommodate diverse user values rather than imposing a single objective. Future work can build personalized AI assistants that adapt to individual or demographic preference groups while maintaining theoretical guarantees on performance. The aggregation approach opens avenues for developing preference-agnostic reward models that robustly combine conflicting feedback without assuming consensus. Applications include fine-tuning systems for diverse user bases, handling value pluralism in alignment, and designing incentive-compatible feedback collection mechanisms that discourage strategic misreporting. This also points toward understanding fundamental limits of what alignment objectives are achievable from heterogeneous feedback and how to navigate value tradeoffs explicitly.
